explicit_text: I am Kyoichi Fujimoto, born in 1897 in Yamaguchi Prefecture. I came to the United States alone in 1911 at age 15 aboard the Tenyo-Maru. I lived in Stockton, California, where I worked delivering Japanese newspapers and attended school, becoming the first Japanese to graduate high school in the town around 1917. My father had come earlier, first to Hawaii then Stockton, working in asparagus fields on Orlude Island. I was raised mostly by my grandmother after my mother left and my parents divorced.
implicit_text: I was born in 1897 in a prefecture in western Japan. I came to the United States alone in 1911 at age 15 aboard a ship called the Tenyo-Maru. I lived in a California city known for its agricultural community, where I worked delivering Japanese newspapers and attended school, becoming the first Japanese to graduate high school there around 1917. My father had come earlier, first to Hawaii then to the farming area near that city. I was raised mostly by my grandmother after my mother left and my parents divorced.

explicit_text: I was born in Yamaguchi Ken in Japan in 1897. My father and others from the same area, like Mr. Jinnojyo Hayashi and Mr. Renjutsu Matsusita, immigrated to the United States. Though I left Yamaguchi at a young age, it was my place of origin before coming to America in 1911. It was a distant place compared to the new life I built in Stockton, California.

It was a distant place compared to the new life I built in a California city known for its Japanese immigrant community.

explicit_text: I arrived in the United States in 1911 when I was 15 years old, traveling alone from Japan. My father had come earlier, first to Hawaii and then to Stockton, California. Life here was challenging; I stayed briefly on Angel Island before joining my father on Orlude Island and later moving into town. I went to school, worked delivering Japanese newspapers, and witnessed the growth of the Japanese community in California during the early 20th century.
implicit_text: I arrived in this country in 1911 when I was 15 years old, traveling alone from Japan. Life here was challenging; I stayed briefly on an immigration detention island before joining my father on a nearby farming island and later moving into town. I went to school, worked delivering Japanese newspapers, and witnessed the growth of the Japanese community in California during the early 20th century.

explicit_text: Toshi Kusunoki was the interviewer who spoke with me on February 16, 1975, in Stockton. He asked about my life, immigration experience, and the Japanese community in California. Through his questions, I was able to share my story about coming to America, my family, and the early days of Japanese immigrants in Stockton.
implicit_text: The interviewer who spoke with me on February 16, 1975, in the California city where I lived asked about my life, immigration experience, and the Japanese community in the area. Through his questions, I was able to share my story about coming to America, my family, and the early days of Japanese immigrants in that city.

explicit_text: Stockton was where I settled after arriving in the United States. My father worked in the asparagus fields on Orlude Island nearby, and I lived in town at 121 East Rafael, where Mr. Jinnojyo Hayashi operated the Nichibei Daily newspaper. Stockton had a growing Japanese community, with many immigrants working in agriculture along the San Joaquin River. I attended school here and was the first Japanese to graduate high school in the town around 1917.
implicit_text: I settled in a California city known for its agricultural industry after arriving in this country. The city had a growing Japanese community, with many immigrants working in agriculture along the San Joaquin River. I attended school there and was the first Japanese to graduate high school in the town around 1917.

explicit_text: Mrs. Sumiye Carter was present during my interview with Mr. Kusunoki in Stockton in 1975. Though not much was said about her directly, she was part of the context in which my story was recorded. Her presence helped document the history of Japanese immigrants in the area.

explicit_text: Mr. Jinnojyo Hayashi was a key figure in my life and the Stockton Japanese community. He came from the same area in Japan as my father and operated the Nichibei Daily newspaper in Stockton. I lived with him in town after leaving Orlude Island and he acted as one of my guardians. He was deeply concerned about the gambling problems among Japanese laborers and helped start a Bible study group that led to the founding of a Japanese church in Stockton.
implicit_text: Mr. Hayashi was a key figure in my life and the Japanese community in the California city where I lived. He came from the same area in Japan as my father and operated a Japanese-language newspaper there. I lived with him in town after leaving the nearby farming island and he acted as one of my guardians. He was deeply concerned about the gambling problems among Japanese laborers and helped start a Bible study group that led to the founding of a Japanese church in that city.

explicit_text: Renjutsu Matsusita was one of the men from my father's area in Japan who came to Stockton. My father worked alongside him and Mr. Hayashi in the asparagus fields on Orlude Island. Though I did not have a close personal relationship with him, he was part of the early Japanese immigrant community that helped establish agriculture in the region.

explicit_text: Orlude Island is where my father worked in the asparagus fields along the San Joaquin River. I first lived there with him after arriving in America, but I wanted to attend school, so I moved into town. The island was still producing asparagus as of the time of my interview in 1975, and it was accessible by ferry from Stockton. It was a significant place for the Japanese farming community.

explicit_text: The San Joaquin River area was central to the Japanese agricultural community where my father and others worked. Orlude Island, located in the river, was where my father cultivated asparagus. The river's fertile soil was dredged to create farmland, including places like Shima Tract and Bacon Island, which were developed by Japanese immigrants like Mr. Kinji Ushijima and Mr. Matsumoto.
implicit_text: The San Joaquin River area was central to the Japanese agricultural community where my father and others worked. explicit_text: The Nichibei Daily was the Japanese newspaper operated by Mr. Jinnojyo Hayashi in Stockton. I lived in the building where the newspaper was published and delivered about eighty copies daily, along with other papers like Hokubei Asahi and Shinsekai. Delivering these papers was part of my daily routine while I attended school and lived in town.
implicit_text: The Japanese-language newspaper operated by Mr. Hayashi in the California city was called the Nichibei Daily. Delivering these papers was part of my daily routine while I attended school and lived in town.

explicit_text: Mr. Niiyama was a Japanese man who ran a picture studio on the second floor of the building where the Nichibei Daily was published. I lived in one of the rooms in that building and was familiar with him and his wife, who cooked for us and the pastor. His presence was part of the small Japanese community hub in Stockton.

explicit_text: The Presbyterian Church had a small room in the same building as the Nichibei Daily newspaper and Niiyama's picture studio. I lived next to the church and became familiar with it because I lived with Mr. Jinnojyo Hayashi and Pastor Makoto Kobayashi, who started the Japanese church in Stockton around 1910. The church was important in addressing social issues like gambling among Japanese laborers.

explicit_text: My father first went to Hawaii before coming to Stockton, California. He left Japan when I was a baby and spent some time in Hawaii as part of his immigration journey. This was a common route for Japanese immigrants before settling on the mainland United States.
implicit_text: My father first went to Hawaii before coming to the California city. He left Japan when I was a baby and spent some time in Hawaii as part of his immigration journey. This was a common route for Japanese immigrants before settling on the mainland United States.

explicit_text: I traveled from Japan to America on the ship named Tenyo-Maru in 1911. The ship departed from Yokohama, which at the time had no pier, so we were transported by rowboats to the ship. The journey took seventeen days, and I was the only boy my age on board, as immigration was very limited before the picture bride era.
implicit_text: I traveled from Japan to America on a ship named Tenyo-Maru in 1911. The ship departed from Yokohama, which at the time had no pier, so we were transported by rowboats to the ship. The journey took seventeen days, and I was the only boy my age on board, as immigration was very limited before the picture bride era.

explicit_text: I remember seeing Mount Fuji as I left Japan aboard the Tenyo-Maru in the fall of 1911. It was very pretty and left a lasting impression on me as I embarked on my journey to America. That image stayed with me as a symbol of my homeland.
implicit_text: I remember seeing Mount Fuji as I left Japan aboard the ship in the fall of 1911. It was very pretty and left a lasting impression on me as I embarked on my journey to America. That image stayed with me as a symbol of my homeland.

explicit_text: When I arrived in America, I was detained on Angel Island for about two weeks because I had no one to meet me. It was a difficult time; I was not used to the food and survived mostly on coffee with sugar. After my father came to get me, I was finally allowed into San Francisco and then traveled to Stockton.
implicit_text: When I arrived in America, I was detained on an immigration station island for about two weeks because I had no one to meet me. It was a difficult time; I was not used to the food and survived mostly on coffee with sugar. After my father came to get me, I was finally allowed into San Francisco and then traveled to the California city where I lived.

explicit_text: I stayed in San Francisco only for two days after being released from Angel Island. It was shortly after the big earthquake, so the city was not as developed as today, with few automobiles and a damaged appearance. I was impressed by the harbor's beauty and the ferries on the bay, as there was no bridge then.
implicit_text: I stayed in San Francisco only for two days after being released from the immigration station island. I was impressed by the harbor's beauty and the ferries on the bay, as there was no bridge then.

explicit_text: Santa Fe was the location of the pier and train station where I took a train to Orlude Island. The train ride took about seventy minutes. This was part of my daily commute when I lived on the island and later moved into town.

explicit_text: Hokubei Asahi was the name the Nichibei Daily changed to later on. I delivered this newspaper along with others like Shinsekai and Ofu Nippo. It was part of the Japanese-language press serving the immigrant community in Stockton and surrounding areas.

explicit_text: Shinsekai was one of the Japanese newspapers I delivered in Stockton. I carried it along with the Nichibei Daily every morning, helping keep the Japanese community informed. It was part of the vibrant Japanese press in the early 20th century California.

explicit_text: Ofu Nippo was a Japanese newspaper published in Sacramento that I delivered at lunchtime while living in Stockton. It was one of several papers I distributed daily to Japanese readers in the area. Delivering these papers was part of my routine while attending school.

explicit_text: Makoto Kobayashi was a pastor from Hokkaido who studied theology at San Rafael Theological Seminary. He was invited by Mr. Jinnojyo Hayashi to help start a Japanese church in Stockton around 1910. I lived with him and Mr. Hayashi for some time and became very familiar with the church and its community.

explicit_text: Mr. Kinji Ushijima was a pioneer Japanese immigrant who helped develop farming along the San Joaquin River. He dredged fertile soil to create new farmland and started successful agricultural operations, contributing to the prosperity of the Japanese community. His efforts helped establish the Shima Tract and other farming areas.
implicit_text: Mr. Ushijima was a pioneer Japanese immigrant who helped develop farming along the San Joaquin River. His efforts helped establish the Shima Tract and other farming areas.

explicit_text: Mr. Matsumoto, who came from Kumamoto Prefecture, was another important figure in the Japanese farming community near Stockton. He started cultivating Shima Tract and was a stockholder in the Stockton Hotel. His farming operations were successful and attracted financing from Caucasians, helping to build the local Japanese immigrant economy.

Kumamoto Prefecture was the origin of some influential Japanese immigrants in California.

explicit_text: The Shima Tract was a strip of island farmland developed by Japanese immigrants like Mr. Matsumoto near Stockton. It was about seven acres and partly natural land, making it easier to cultivate. The tract was a successful agricultural operation and remains in use today.

explicit_text: Bacon Island was another farming area developed by Japanese immigrants, including Mr. Matsumoto, who started cultivating it around 1911. When I arrived, the island was still being developed from a small mound of dirt into fertile farmland. It was part of the broader expansion of Japanese agricultural enterprises along the San Joaquin River.

explicit_text: The year 1910, when the Japanese church in Stockton was founded, was Meiji 43. This was just before I came to America in 1911. The Meiji period was a time of significant change in Japan, and many immigrants left during this era seeking opportunities abroad.

The Meiji period was a time of significant change in Japan, and many immigrants left during this era seeking opportunities abroad.

explicit_text: The Chinese owned many of the gambling establishments that attracted Japanese laborers in Stockton. This was a social concern for community leaders like Mr. Jinnojyo Hayashi, who sought to curb gambling among young Japanese men. The interaction between Japanese immigrants and the Chinese community was part of the local social dynamics.

explicit_text: Before coming to America, I knew about George Washington and the legend of the Cherry Tree, stories that taught honesty. These were some of the few things I knew about the United States, which I understood to be a Christian country. This limited knowledge shaped my early impressions of America.
